title: Intermittency and metastable dark states as a resource for continuous sensing
authors: Robert Mattes, Igor Lesanovsky, Albert Cabot
abstract: Quanta emitted by an open quantum system carry information about intrinsic parameters, enabling their estimation via continuous monitoring. In practice, however, only a fraction of the emitted quanta is detected, reducing the achievable sensitivity. Here, we consider few-level systems in which coherent couplings and dissipative processes compete, producing metastable dynamics characterized by emission intermittency or by the emergence of a dark state. We show that both phenomena can be beneficial for sensing but their relative performance depends strongly on the achievable detection efficiencies. Intermittent emission, marked by long alternating bright and dark periods, allows to achieve robustness with respect to inefficient detection and dephasing, whereas dark states yield significantly higher sensitivity at unit detection efficiency. Yet the latter are highly susceptible to losses. We quantify the impact of inefficient detection through the classical Fisher information of the emission record and benchmark it against the ultimate sensitivity encoded in the joint system-environment state. Finally, we demonstrate that maximum-likelihood estimators based on the observed emission record can effectively approach this sensitivity. We focus here on trapped-ion systems, however, the results extend to other quantum platforms in which similar emission dynamics can be observed.
summary: This paper compares two types of quantum emission dynamics—intermittency and metastable dark states—as resources for continuous parameter sensing. It reveals that while dark states offer superior sensitivity in ideal conditions, intermittent 'blinking' dynamics are far more resilient to the detection losses and dephasing common in real-world experiments.
main_problem: Evaluating how different emission dynamics, specifically intermittency (blinking) and metastable dark states, impact the sensitivity of continuous quantum sensing under detection inefficiency and dephasing.
main_result: Metastable dark states provide much higher sensitivity at unit detection efficiency but are highly sensitive to losses, whereas intermittent emission is less sensitive but much more robust to low detection efficiency and dephasing.
method: The study uses the Lindblad master equation, classical and quantum Fisher information (FI and QFI) to quantify sensitivity, and Maximum-Likelihood Estimators (MLE) to evaluate parameter estimation performance.
model_or_system: A four-level driven-dissipative system (applicable to trapped ions like Sr+ and Ca+) featuring a strong fluorescent transition and a weak metastable transition, modeled via a Lindblad master equation.
key_observables: Photon-counting records, waiting time distributions (WTD), and the no-jump probability.
important_parameters: Detection efficiency (eta_g), Rabi frequencies (Omega_e, Omega_d, Omega_u), detuning (Delta_u), and dephasing rate (gamma_D).
main_assumptions: The system is treated as a renewal process where every detected jump returns the system to the ground state; the analysis focuses on the asymptotic regime of long observation times.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the level scheme and WTD for different setups; Figure 2 compares WTD parameters and stationary populations; Figure 3 and 4 analyze FI rates and WTD oscillations; Figure 12 shows the damping of WTD modulations due to dephasing.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the sensing problem and two dynamical setups, analyzes the waiting time distributions and sensitivity (FI/QFI) for both regimes, evaluates the impact of experimental imperfections like detection loss and dephasing, and demonstrates the optimality of MLE-based estimation.
why_interesting: It provides a critical trade-off analysis between high-sensitivity/high-fragility (dark states) and low-sensitivity/high-robustness (intermittency) regimes, which is essential for designing practical continuous-monitoring quantum sensors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
